**Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 234 When you encounter particularly archaic or esoteric texts, the GM might require you to Decipher the Writing before you can understand it. You must be trained in the relevant skill to Decipher Writing. Arcana is typically used for writing about magic or science, Occultism for esoteric texts about mysteries and philosophy, Religion for scripture, and Society for coded messages or archaic documents. Skill Uses [[Arcana](/Skills.aspx?ID=2)] Decipher Writing about arcane theory. [[Occultism](/Skills.aspx?ID=11)] Decipher Writing on occult topics, including complex metaphysical systems, syncretic principles, weird philosophies, and incoherent ramblings. [[Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=13)] Decipher Writing of a religious nature, including allegories, homilies, and proverbs. [[Society](/Skills.aspx?ID=14)] Decipher Writing that’s a coded message, text written in an incomplete or archaic form, or in some cases, text in a language you don’t know. Item Bonuses for Decipher Writing
Item Bonus Level Category Consumable Note
[Spectacles of Understanding](/Equipment.aspx?ID=882) +1 5 [Other Worn Items](/equipment/worn-items/other-worn-items) No
[Conundrum Spectacles](/Equipment.aspx?ID=1468) +1 5 [Other Worn Items](/equipment/worn-items/other-worn-items) No
[Conundrum Spectacles (Greater)](/Equipment.aspx?ID=1468) +2 10 [Other Worn Items](/equipment/worn-items/other-worn-items) No
[Spectacles of Understanding (Greater)](/Equipment.aspx?ID=882) +2 11 [Other Worn Items](/equipment/worn-items/other-worn-items) No
[Whispering Staff](/Equipment.aspx?ID=2146) +3 20 [Staves](/equipment/staves) No
[Whispering Staff](/Equipment.aspx?ID=4679) +3 20 [Apex Items](/equipment/apex-items) No Item bonus to Decipher Writing regardless of the skill
[Whisperer of Souls](/Equipment.aspx?ID=4896) +4 28 [Artifacts](/equipment/artifacts) No Item bonus to Decipher Writing regardless of the skill

markdown:
**Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 235 You can use a skill—typically Crafting, Lore, or Performance—to earn money during downtime. You must be trained in the skill to do so. This takes time to set up, and your income depends on your proficiency rank and how lucrative a task you can find. Because this process requires a significant amount of time and involves tracking things outside the progress of adventures, it won’t come up in every campaign. In some cases, the GM might let you use a different skill to Earn Income through specialized work. Usually, this is scholarly work, such as using Religion in a monastery to study old texts—but giving sermons at a church would still fall under Performance instead of Religion. You also might be able to use physical skills to make money, such as using Acrobatics to perform feats in a circus or Thievery to pick pockets. If you’re using a skill other than Crafting, Lore, or Performance, the DC tends to be significantly higher. [Earn Income Calculator](/earn-income-calculator) Skill Uses [[Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=4)] Earn Income by crafting goods for the market. [[Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=8)] Earn Income by using your knowledge to practice a trade. [[Performance](/Skills.aspx?ID=12)] Earn Income by staging a performance. Item Bonuses for Earn Income
Item Bonus Level Category Consumable Note
[Fortune Cord](/Equipment.aspx?ID=3169) +2 11 [Held Items](/equipment/held-items) No
[Fortune Cord](/Equipment.aspx?ID=4089) +2 11 [Held Items](/equipment/held-items) No Item bonus to Earn Income
[Hat of Many Minds](/Equipment.aspx?ID=1060) +2 12 [Other Worn Items](/equipment/worn-items/other-worn-items) No
Income ExamplesThe following examples show the kinds of tasks your character might take on to Earn Income during low-level and high-level play. Harsk Makes Tea Harsk is a 3rd-level ranger and an expert at harvesting and brewing tea. He has a Tea Lore modifier of +7. He has 30 days of downtime at his disposal and decides to work at a prestigious local tea house. The GM decides this is a 5th-level task if Harsk wants to assist the tea master, or a 2nd-level task if he wants to serve tea. Harsk chooses the tougher task, and the GM secretly sets the DC at 20. Harsk rolls a 4 on his Tea Lore check for a result of 11. Poor Harsk has failed! He earns only 2 sp for his efforts and continues working for 3 more days, for a total of 8 sp. At that point, the GM offers Harsk a choice: either he can finish out the week with the tea master and look for a new job, or he can lower his ambitions and serve in the tea house. Harsk, now more aware of his own capabilities, accepts the less prestigious job for now. He moves to his new job and attempts a new Tea Lore check against DC 16. Rolling a 19, he gets a result of 26—a critical success! He earns 5 sp per day (like a success at a 3rd-level task). The GM rules that demand will be high enough that Harsk can work there for the remainder of his downtime if he so chooses, a total of 26 days. Harsk accepts and earns a total of 138 sp (13 gp, 8 sp) that month. Lem Performs Lem is a 16th-level bard and legendary with his flute. He has a Performance modifier of +31 with his enchanted flute. With 30 days of downtime ahead of him, Lem wonders if he can find something that might excite him more than performing in front of a bunch of stuffy nobles. He finds a momentous offer indeed—a performance in a celestial realm, and Lem's patron goddess Shelyn might even be in attendance! This is a 20th-level task, and the GM secretly sets the DC at 40. Lem rolls an 11 on his Performance check for a result of 42. Success! The engagement lasts for a week, and at the end, the grateful celestials present Lem with a beautiful living diamond rose in constant bloom worth 1,400 gold pieces (200 gp per day for 7 days). With 23 days of downtime left, Lem accepts a 14th-level task performing at a prestigious bardic college for members of a royal court. The GM secretly sets the DC at 32, and Lem critically succeeds, earning 28 gp per day for a total of 644 gp. Between the two performances, Lem has earned just over 2,000 gold pieces during his downtime—though he's not sure he'll ever sell that rose. Extra Preparation When Earning Income, you might be able to spend days of downtime to prepare for your task, which adjusts the DC of the skill check. This might involve rehearsing a play, studying a topic, and so on. The GM determines how long preparation takes and how much the DC changes. This is most useful when you're trying a task that's higher level than you; otherwise such tasks have an increased DC! Ending Or Interrupting Tasks When a task you're doing is complete, or if you stop in the middle of one, you normally have to find a new task if you want to keep Earning Income. For instance, if you quit your job working at the docks, you'll need to find another place of employment instead of picking up where you left off. This usually takes 1 day or more of downtime looking for leads on new jobs. However, you might pause a task due to an adventure or event that wouldn't prevent you from returning to the old job later. The GM might decide that you can pick up where you left off, assuming the task hasn't been completed by others in your absence. Whether you roll a new skill check when you resume is also up to the GM. Generally speaking, if you had a good initial roll and want to keep it, you can, but if you had a bad initial roll, you can't try for a better one by pausing to do something else. If your statistics changed during the break—usually because you leveled up while adventuring—you can attempt a new check. Crafting Goods for the Market [[Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=4)] **Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 237 Using Crafting, you can work at producing common items for the market. It’s usually easy to find work making basic items whose level is 1 or 2 below your settlement’s level (see Earn Income on page 504). Higher-level tasks represent special commissions, which might require you to Craft a specific item using the Craft downtime activity and sell it to a buyer at full price. These opportunities don’t occur as often and might have special requirements—or serious consequences if you disappoint a prominent client. Practicing a Trade [[Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=8)] **Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 237 You apply the practical benefits of one of your Lore specialties during downtime by practicing your trade. This is most effective for Lore specialties such as business, law, or sailing, where there’s high demand for workers. The GM might increase the DC or determine only low-level tasks are available if you’re attempting to use an obscure Lore skill to Earn Income. You might also need specialized tools to accept a job, like mining tools to work in a mine or a merchant’s scale to buy and sell valuables in a market. Staging a Performance [[Performance](/Skills.aspx?ID=12)] **Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 238 You perform for an audience to make money. The available audiences determine the level of your task, since more discerning audiences are harder to impress but provide a bigger payout. The GM determines the task level based on the audiences available. Performing for a typical audience of commoners on the street is a level 0 task, but a performance for a group of artisans with more refined tastes might be a 2nd- or 3rd-level task, and ones for merchants, nobility, and royalty are increasingly higher level. Your degree of success determines whether you moved your audience and whether you were rewarded with applause or rotten fruit.

Staging a Performance [Performance] Source Core Rulebook pg. 238 You perform for an audience to make money. The available audiences determine the level of your task, since more discerning audiences are harder to impress but provide a bigger payout. The GM determines the task level based on the audiences available. Performing for a typical audience of commoners on the street is a level 0 task, but a performance for a group of artisans with more refined tastes might be a 2nd- or 3rd-level task, and ones for merchants, nobility, and royalty are increasingly higher level. Your degree of success determines whether you moved your audience and whether you were rewarded with applause or rotten fruit.

<title level="1" right="General Skill Action " pfs="Standard">[Earn Income](/Skills.aspx?ID=21&General=true) (Trained)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 228

You can use a skill to earn money during downtime. You must be trained in the skill to do so. This takes time to set up, and your income depends on your proficiency rank and how lucrative a task you can find. Because this process requires a significant amount of time and involves tracking things outside the progress of adventures, it won't come up in every campaign.

In some cases, the GM might let you use a different skill to Earn Income through specialized work. Usually, this is scholarly work, such as using [Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=13) in a monastery to study old texts—but giving sermons at a church would still fall under [Performance](/Skills.aspx?ID=12) instead of Religion. You also might be able to use physical skills to make money, such as using [Acrobatics](/Skills.aspx?ID=1) to perform feats in a circus or Thievery to pick pockets. If you're using a skill other than [Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=4), [Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=8), or Performance, the DC tends to be significantly higher. 

[Earn Income Calculator](/earn-income-calculator)

<title level="2">Skill Uses</title>
[[Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=37)] Earn Income by crafting goods for the market.

[[Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=41)] Earn Income by using your knowledge to practice a trade.

[[Performance](/Skills.aspx?ID=45)] Earn Income by staging a performance.

<title level="2" right="">Income Examples</title>The following examples show the kinds of tasks your character might take on to Earn Income during low-level and high-level play. 

<title level="3" right="">Harsk Makes Tea</title> Harsk is a 3rd-level ranger and an expert at harvesting and brewing tea. He has a Tea Lore modifier of +7. He has 30 days of downtime at his disposal and decides to work at a prestigious local tea house. The GM decides this is a 5th-level task if Harsk wants to assist the tea master, or a 2nd-level task if he wants to serve tea. Harsk chooses the tougher task, and the GM secretly sets the DC at 20.

 Harsk rolls a 4 on his Tea Lore check for a result of 11. Poor Harsk has failed! He earns only 2 sp for his efforts and continues working for 3 more days, for a total of 8 sp.

 At that point, the GM offers Harsk a choice: either he can finish out the week with the tea master and look for a new job, or he can lower his ambitions and serve in the tea house. Harsk, now more aware of his own capabilities, accepts the less prestigious job for now. He moves to his new job and attempts a new Tea Lore check against DC 16. Rolling a 19, he gets a result of 26—a critical success! He earns 5 sp per day (like a success at a 3rd-level task). The GM rules that demand will be high enough that Harsk can work there for the remainder of his downtime if he so chooses, a total of 26 days. Harsk accepts and earns a total of 138 sp (13 gp, 8 sp) that month. 

<title level="3" right="">Lem Performs</title> Lem is a 16th-level bard and legendary with his flute. He has a Performance modifier of +31 with his enchanted flute. With 30 days of downtime ahead of him, Lem wonders if he can find something that might excite him more than performing in front of a bunch of stuffy nobles. He finds a momentous offer indeed—a performance in a celestial realm, and Lem's patron goddess Shelyn might even be in attendance! This is a 20th-level task, and the GM secretly sets the DC at 40.

 Lem rolls an 11 on his Performance check for a result of 42. Success! The engagement lasts for a week, and at the end, the grateful celestials present Lem with a beautiful living diamond rose in constant bloom worth 1,400 gold pieces (200 gp per day for 7 days).

 With 23 days of downtime left, Lem accepts a 14th-level task performing at a prestigious bardic college for members of a royal court. The GM secretly sets the DC at 32, and Lem critically succeeds, earning 28 gp per day for a total of 644 gp. Between the two performances, Lem has earned just over 2,000 gold pieces during his downtime—though he's not sure he'll ever sell that rose.

<title level="2" right="">Extra Preparation</title> When Earning Income, you might be able to spend days of downtime to prepare for your task, which adjusts the DC of the skill check. This might involve rehearsing a play, studying a topic, and so on. The GM determines how long preparation takes and how much the DC changes. This is most useful when you're trying a task that's higher level than you; otherwise such tasks have an increased DC! 

<title level="2" right="">Ending Or Interrupting Tasks</title> When a task you're doing is complete, or if you stop in the middle of one, you normally have to find a new task if you want to keep Earning Income. For instance, if you quit your job working at the docks, you'll need to find another place of employment instead of picking up where you left off. This usually takes 1 day or more of downtime looking for leads on new jobs.

 However, you might pause a task due to an adventure or event that wouldn't prevent you from returning to the old job later. The GM might decide that you can pick up where you left off, assuming the task hasn't been completed by others in your absence. Whether you roll a new skill check when you resume is also up to the GM. Generally speaking, if you had a good initial roll and want to keep it, you can, but if you had a bad initial roll, you can't try for a better one by pausing to do something else. If your statistics changed during the break—usually because you leveled up while adventuring—you can attempt a new check.

<document level="2" id="action-2364" />

<title level="2">Crafting Goods for the Market [[Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=37)]</title>

**Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 229

Using [Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=4), you can work at producing common items for the market. It's usually easy to find work making basic items whose level is 1 or 2 below your settlement's level.

 Higher-level tasks represent special commissions, which might require you to [Craft](/Actions.aspx?ID=43) a specific item using the Craft downtime activity and sell it to a buyer at full Price. These opportunities don't occur as often and might have special requirements—or serious consequences if you disappoint a prominent client.

<title level="2">Practicing a Trade [[Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=41)]</title>

**Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 230

You apply the practical benefits of one of your [Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=8) specialties during downtime by practicing your trade. This is most effective for Lore specialties such as business, law, or sailing, where there's high demand for workers. The GM might increase the DC or determine only low-level tasks are available if you're attempting to use an obscure Lore skill to Earn Income. You might also need specialized tools to accept a job, like mining tools to work in a mine or a merchant's scale to buy and sell valuables in a market.

<title level="2">Staging a Performance [[Performance](/Skills.aspx?ID=45)]</title>

**Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 230

You perform for an audience to make money. The available audiences determine the level of your task, since more discerning audiences are harder to impress but provide a bigger payout. The GM determines the task level based on the audiences available. Performing for a typical audience of commoners on the street is a level 0 task, but a performance for a group of artisans with more refined tastes might be a 2nd- or 3rd-level task, and ones for merchants, nobility, and royalty are increasingly higher level.

 Your degree of success determines whether you moved your audience and whether you were rewarded with applause or rotten fruit.

<title level="1" right="General Skill Action " pfs="Standard">[Identify Magic](/Skills.aspx?ID=22&General=true) (Trained)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 230

Using the skill related to the appropriate tradition, as explained in the Magical Traditions and Skills sidebar, you can attempt to identify a magical item, location, or ongoing effect. In many cases, you can use a skill to attempt to Identify Magic of a tradition other than your own at a higher DC. The GM determines whether you can do this and what the DC is.

<title level="2">Skill Uses</title>
[[Arcana](/Skills.aspx?ID=35)] Identify Magic , particularly magic of the [arcane](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=1) tradition.

[[Nature](/Skills.aspx?ID=43)] Identify Magic , particularly magic of the [primal](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=4) tradition.

[[Occultism](/Skills.aspx?ID=44)] Identify Magic , particularly magic of the [occult](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=3) tradition.

[[Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=46)] Identify Magic , particularly magic of the [divine](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=2) tradition.

<title level="1" right="General Skill Action " pfs="Standard">[Learn a Spell](/Skills.aspx?ID=23&General=true) (Trained)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 230

If you're a spellcaster, you can use the skill corresponding to your magical tradition to learn a new spell of that tradition. The Learning a Spell table lists the Price of the materials needed to Learn a Spell of each rank. Learning a Spell is most useful for classes that use a limited list of spells, like the [bard](/Classes.aspx?ID=3), [witch](/Classes.aspx?ID=16), or [wizard](/Classes.aspx?ID=12), though other classes might use it to gain access to [rare](/Traits.aspx?ID=137) or [uncommon](/Traits.aspx?ID=159) spells.

<title level="2">Skill Uses</title>
[[Arcana](/Skills.aspx?ID=35)] Learn a Spell from the [arcane](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=1) tradition.

[[Nature](/Skills.aspx?ID=43)] Learn a Spell from the [primal](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=4) tradition.

[[Occultism](/Skills.aspx?ID=44)] Learn a Spell from the [occult](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=3) tradition.

[[Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=46)] Learn a Spell from the [divine](/SpellLists.aspx?Tradition=2) tradition.

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 231 To remember useful information on a topic, you can attempt to Recall Knowledge. This action is one you're likely to use frequently. Learning more about the world and people around you is one of the best ways to inform your decisions, and Recalling Knowledge can help you figure out how to best fight monsters and figure out puzzles and social challenges. You might know basic information about something without needing to attempt a check, but Recall Knowledge requires you to stop and think for a moment so you can recollect more specific facts and apply them. You might even need to spend time investigating first. For instance, to use [Medicine](/Skills.aspx?ID=9) to learn the cause of death, you might need to conduct a forensic examination before attempting to Recall Knowledge. Skill Uses [[Arcana](/Skills.aspx?ID=35)] Recall Knowledge about arcane theories; magic traditions; creatures of arcane significance (like [constructs](/Traits.aspx?ID=563), [beasts](/Traits.aspx?ID=547), and [elementals](/Traits.aspx?ID=587)); and the [Astral Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=8), Elemental Planes, and [the Netherworld](/Planes.aspx?ID=36). [[Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=37)] Recall Knowledge about alchemical reactions, the value of items, engineering, unusual materials, and alchemical or mechanical creatures. The GM determines which creatures this applies to, but it usually includes [constructs](/Traits.aspx?ID=35). [[Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=41)] Recall Knowledge about the subject of the Lore skill's subcategory. [[Medicine](/Skills.aspx?ID=42)] Recall Knowledge about diseases, injuries, poisons, and other ailments. You can use this to perform forensic examinations if you spend 10 minutes (or more, as determined by the GM) checking for evidence such as wound patterns. This is most useful when determining how a body was injured or killed. [[Nature](/Skills.aspx?ID=43)] Recall Knowledge about fauna, flora, geography, weather, the environment, creatures of natural origin (like [animals](/Traits.aspx?ID=531), [beasts](/Traits.aspx?ID=547), [fey](/Traits.aspx?ID=69), and [plants](/Traits.aspx?ID=668)), the [First World](/Planes.aspx?ID=10), and the Elemental Planes. [[Occultism](/Skills.aspx?ID=44)] Recall Knowledge about ancient mysteries; obscure philosophies; creatures of occult significance (like [aberrations](/Traits.aspx?ID=523), [spirits](/Traits.aspx?ID=149), and [oozes](/Traits.aspx?ID=665)); and esoteric planes like the [Astral Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=8), [Creation's Forge](/Planes.aspx?ID=27), the [Ethereal Plane](/Planes.aspx?ID=9), [the Netherworld](/Planes.aspx?ID=36), and [the Void](/Planes.aspx?ID=28). [[Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=46)] Recall Knowledge about divine agents, the finer points of theology, obscure myths regarding a faith, and creatures of religious significance (like [celestials](/Traits.aspx?ID=551), [fiends](/Traits.aspx?ID=600), and [undead](/Traits.aspx?ID=160)), [Creation's Forge](/Planes.aspx?ID=27), [the Void](/Planes.aspx?ID=28), and the Outer Sphere. [[Society](/Skills.aspx?ID=47)] Recall Knowledge about local history, important personalities, legal institutions, societal structure, and humanoid cultures. The GM might allow Society to apply to other creatures that are major elements of society in your region, such as the draconic nobility in a kingdom of humans ruled by dragons. Item Bonuses for Recall Knowledge
Item Bonus Level Category Consumable Note
[Mnemonic Feather](/Equipment.aspx?ID=3523) +1 3 [Held Items](/equipment/held-items) No Attempts to Recall Knowledge about a topic contained within the book that the feather is placed in
[Hoax-Hunter's Kit](/Equipment.aspx?ID=4115) +1 3 [Adventuring Gear](/equipment/adventuring-gear) No Recall Knowledge about a creature you are studying
[Echo Token](/Equipment.aspx?ID=3521) +1 1 [Other Consumables](/equipment/consumables/other-consumables) Yes A specific type of Lore
[Krasovnatype](/Equipment.aspx?ID=3566) +1 4 [Gadgets](/equipment/consumables/gadgets) Yes Recall Knowledge check regarding the creature imprinted.
[Memory Ribbon](/Equipment.aspx?ID=3768) +2 4 [Other Consumables](/equipment/consumables/other-consumables) Yes Item bonus to Recall Knowledge

[Religion] Recall Knowledge about divine agents, the finer points of theology, obscure myths regarding a faith, and creatures of religious significance (like celestials, fiends, and undead), Creation's Forge, the Void, and the Outer Sphere. [Society] Recall Knowledge about local history, important personalities, legal institutions, societal structure, and humanoid cultures. The GM might allow Society to apply to other creatures that are major elements of society in your region, such as the draconic nobility in a kingdom of humans ruled by dragons. **Source** [Core Rulebook](/Sources.aspx?ID=1) pg. 238 To remember useful information on a topic, you can attempt to Recall Knowledge. You might know basic information about something without needing to attempt a check, but Recall Knowledge requires you to stop and think for a moment so you can recollect more specific facts and apply them. You might even need to spend time investigating first. For instance, to use Medicine to learn the cause of death, you might need to conduct a forensic examination before attempting to Recall Knowledge. Skill Uses [[Arcana](/Skills.aspx?ID=2)] Recall Knowledge about arcane theories; magic traditions; creatures of arcane significance (like dragons and beasts); and the Elemental, Astral, and Shadow Planes. [[Crafting](/Skills.aspx?ID=4)] Recall Knowledge about alchemical reactions, the value of items, engineering, unusual materials, and alchemical or mechanical creatures. The GM determines which creatures this applies to, but it usually includes constructs. [[Lore](/Skills.aspx?ID=8)] Recall Knowledge about the subject of your Lore skill’s subcategory. [[Nature](/Skills.aspx?ID=10)] Recall Knowledge about fauna, flora, geography, weather, the environment, creatures of natural origin (like animals, beasts, fey, and plants), the First World, the Material Plane, and the Elemental Planes. [[Occultism](/Skills.aspx?ID=11)] Recall Knowledge about ancient mysteries; obscure philosophies; creatures of occult significance (like aberrations, spirits, and oozes); and the Positive Energy, Negative Energy, Shadow, Astral, and Ethereal Planes. [[Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=13)] Recall Knowledge about divine agents, the finer points of theology, obscure myths regarding a faith, and creatures of religious significance (like celestials, fiends, and undead), the Outer Sphere, and the Positive and Negative Energy Planes. [[Society](/Skills.aspx?ID=14)] Recall Knowledge about local history, important personalities, legal institutions, societal structure, and humanoid cultures. The GM might allow Society to apply to other creatures that are major elements of society in your region, such as the draconic nobility in a kingdom of humans ruled by dragons. [[Medicine](/Skills.aspx?ID=9)] Recall Knowledge about diseases, injuries, poisons, and other ailments. You can use this to perform forensic examinations if you spend 10 minutes (or more, as determined by the GM) checking for evidence such as wound patterns. This is most useful when determining how a body was injured or killed. Item Bonuses for Recall Knowledge

text: Subsist (Untrained) Source Core Rulebook pg. 238 If you need to provide food and shelter, you can use the Subsist downtime activity. This typically uses Society if you’re in a settlement or Survival if you’re in the wild. Skill Uses [Society] Subsist in a settlement by finding shelter, scrounging, or begging for food. [Survival] Subsist in the wild by foraging for food and building shelter.

<title level="1" right="General Skill Action " pfs="Limited">[Tap Ley Line](/Skills.aspx?ID=7&General=true) (Trained)</title>

**Source** [Secrets of Magic](/Sources.aspx?ID=96) pg. 215

You can use your magical prowess to temporarily access the power of a [ley line](/Rules.aspx?ID=1531). Tapping the Ley Line requires a skill check using the skill associated with its tradition ([Arcana](/Skills.aspx?ID=2) for [arcane](/Traits.aspx?ID=11), [Nature](/Skills.aspx?ID=10) for [primal](/Traits.aspx?ID=134), [Occultism](/Skills.aspx?ID=11) for [occult](/Traits.aspx?ID=120), and [Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=13) for [divine](/Traits.aspx?ID=48)). In cases where a ley line has multiple associated traditions, you can use any of the appropriate skills to Tap the Ley Line. The benefits of a ley line are always [magical](/Traits.aspx?ID=103) in nature and can be [counteracted](/Rules.aspx?ID=371) in a similar way to a magic item.

<title level="2">Skill Uses</title>
[[Arcana](/Skills.aspx?ID=2)] Tap Ley Line tapping an arcane ley line

[[Nature](/Skills.aspx?ID=10)] Tap Ley Line tapping a primal ley line

[[Occultism](/Skills.aspx?ID=11)] Tap Ley Line tapping an occult ley line

[[Religion](/Skills.aspx?ID=13)] Tap Ley Line tapping a divine ley line
